coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of Pd nanocones as a novel and effective electrochemiluminescence illuminant for highly sensitive detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hui-Min
Wang, Chen-Chen
Wang, Ai-Jun
Zhang, Lu
Luo, Xiliang
Yuan, Pei-Xin
Feng, Jiu-Ju
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has attracted significant interest because of its superior properties and potential applications in bioanalysis, and therefore many luminescent nanomaterials have been developed. Herein, well-defined Pd nanocones (Pd NCs) were facilely obtained by a one-pot hydrothermal method with poly-l-lysine (PLL) as a green protecting ligand, which were mainly characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Then, the as-obtained Pd NCs were first explored as an anodic ECL emitter, where tripropylamine (TPA) worked as the coreactant to boost the ECL emission. The possible ECL mechanism of Pd NCs/TPA system was extensively explored by ECL and electrochemical techniques. Briefly, the ECL emission underwent the oxidation-reduction route, in which both Pd NCs and TPA were initially oxidized, and then the oxidized TPA lost an proton to form reducing agent (TPA·) strongly reacted with the oxidized Pd NCs generating Pd NCs* for the emission. Furthermore, using dopamine (DA) as an ECL quencher, an ultrasensitive sensor was constructed for DA detection. The fabricated ECL sensor shows high sensitivity and selectivity, good stability, and wide linear range with the low detection limit of 0.46 pM. It offers a new avenue to exploit ECL illuminant and enriches the ECL emission theory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Nanocones
ECL reaction mechanism
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The screening of coreactant and optimization of the detection conditions
The possible ECL reaction mechanism of Pd NCs/TPA and quenching of Pd NCs by DA
ECL performance of the Pd NCs/TPA towards DA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475118 and 21675093 ), Basic Public Welfare Research Project of Zhejiang Province (No. LGG18E010001 ), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201703 , STAM201804 ), the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials (Zhejiang Normal University) , and PhD Fund of Zhejiang Normal University for Young Teachers (Nos. zz323205020517002104 and ZZ323205020518001924 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Sketch diagram of the preparation procedures of Pd NCs and the Pd NCs/TPA system-based ECL biosensor for the assay of DA. (B) The ECL enhancing mechanism of the Pd NCs/TPA system and quenching mechanism of DA in the Pd NCs/TPA system.
Typical low- (A), medium- (B), and high-resolution (C) TEM images of Pd NCs. Survey (D) and high-resolution Pd 3d (E) XPS spectra of Pd NCs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Pd NCs (curve a) and Pd NCs/TPA (curve b). Inset in (A) shows the PL spectra of Pd NCs with different excitation wavelengths. (B) The PL spectra of Pd NCs (curve a), Pd NCs/TPA (curve b), and Pd NCs/TPA/DA (curve c) excited at 350 nm. Inset in part B shows the ECL emission spectrum of Pd NCs with the help of optical filters.

(A) The ECL-potential curves of the GCEs in Pd NCs suspension (10 μg/mL) without (curve a) and with (curve b–d) different coreactants at the concentration of 30 mM in pH 7.0 PBS: TPA (curve b), (B) HZ, and Na2C2O4 (curve c and d). (C) The ECL responses of the GCEs detected in the pH 7.0 PBS containing 10 μg/mL Pd NCs with different TPA concentrations. (D) The ECL responses of the GCEs detected in the PBS containing 10 μg/mL Pd NCs and 30 mM TPA at different pH values.

(A) The ECL-potential curves of the GCEs recorded in the Pd NCs suspension (10 μg/mL) without (curve a) and with 30 mM TPA (curve b), and 30 mM TPA and 1.0 nM DA (curve c). (B) The corresponding CV curves of Pd NCs modified GCE taken in pH 7.0 PBS containing 0.5 M KCl. Insets in A and B display the ECL and electrochemical plots of the coreactant TPA.

(A) ECL intensities of the biosensor with different concentrations of DA: 0, 0.1 pM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM, 1 nM, 10 nM, and 100 nM (curves a–h). (B) The linear calibration curves of the ECL intensity against the logarithm of the DA concentration. (C) The selectivity of the Pd NCs/TPA-based sensing system for the DA detection over other interferences including l-Arg, Glc, His, AA, UA, and Gly. (D) The consecutive scans of the developed Pd NCs/TPA system.
Comparison of this developed biosensor with those in the literature for DA detection.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of Pd nanocones as a novel and effective electrochemiluminescence illuminant for highly sensitive detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials, College of Chemistry and Life Sciences, College of Geography and Environmental Sciences, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua, 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials
College of Chemistry and Life Sciences
College of Geography and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has attracted significant interest because of its superior properties and potential applications in bioanalysis, and therefore many luminescent nanomaterials have been developed. Herein, well-defined Pd nanocones (Pd NCs) were facilely obtained by a one-pot hydrothermal method with poly-l-lysine (PLL) as a green protecting ligand, which were mainly characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Then, the as-obtained Pd NCs were first explored as an anodic ECL emitter, where tripropylamine (TPA) worked as the coreactant to boost the ECL emission. The possible ECL mechanism of Pd NCs/TPA system was extensively explored by ECL and electrochemical techniques. Briefly, the ECL emission underwent the oxidation-reduction route, in which both Pd NCs and TPA were initially oxidized, and then the oxidized TPA lost an proton to form reducing agent (TPA·) strongly reacted with the oxidized Pd NCs generating Pd NCs* for the emission. Furthermore, using dopamine (DA) as an ECL quencher, an ultrasensitive sensor was constructed for DA detection. The fabricated ECL sensor shows high sensitivity and selectivity, good stability, and wide linear range with the low detection limit of 0.46 pM. It offers a new avenue to exploit ECL illuminant and enriches the ECL emission theory.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has gained popularity in clinical diagnosis, food safety and environmental inspection due to its superior optical emission [1]. Especially, ECL platform shows high sensitivity and selectivity, good temporal, spatial control and nearly zero background signals even without extra light source, outperforming other analytical techniques [2]. Efficient chemiluminescent agents include organics (e.g., luminol [3]), inorganics (e.g., ruthenium complexes [4]), and semiconductor nanocrystals [5], which have wide applications in the field of molecular biology [6]. Among them, semiconductor nanocrystalline (e.g., CdS [7], CdSe [8], and CdTe [9]) have high quantum yields and narrow emission band, which usually work as ECL emitter in biosensing [10]. However, their ECL emission usually suffer from inevitable lost and fluctuation in the presence of certain chemical composition degrades or at the single-particle level of quantum dot [5]. Also, environmentally unfriendly and/or high-toxicity of heavy metals in these materials all seriously hamper their potential applications [11].

Fortunately, the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology offers a new route to explore ECL illuminant [12]. Noble metal nanocrystals have gained substantial attention as optical probes in bioanalysis because of their large surface area providing abundant active sites and excellent optical property. Nowadays, these nanocrystals have been introduced as luminescent in biosensor [13], which effectively enhance their emission via the high catalytic activity towards coreactants [14]. Recently, most researches focus on Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) [15] or Ag NCs [16] as ECL emitter, K2S2O8 as the coreactant via the “oxidation-reduction’’ route to effectively enhance the ECL intensity. Besides, the cathodic ECL behavior of upconversion nanopraticles with K2S2O8 is observed via the electron transfer of rare earths from the oxidation-reduction excitation [17]. Lately, Cu NCs are chosen as ECL emitter and hydrazine (HZ) as efficient coreactant to enhance the ECL intensity [18]. Considering the weak ECL emission of the above nanocrystals, it is still significant to search novel candidate with enhanced intensity [19].

Pd nanomaterials are commonly utilized for the electro-oxidation of small organic molecules, owing to their high electrocatalytic activity, good stability and resistance to the poisoning intermediates [20]. Thus, it is feasible for them to transfer electron and energy in photoelectric sensing [21]. Particularly, Pd ions complex with tetraphenylporphin (TPP) to form Pd-TPP, which is explored to emit ECL radiation in non-aqueous systems by annihilation pathway [22]. Inspired by this example, an novel ECL mode was provided here, in which Pd nanocrystals directly delivered electron and energy with coreactants to form the excited states. Unlike the “metal-to-ligand charge-transfer transition’’ [23], the ECL capacity here was mainly determined by the electrical conductivity efficiency and surface electronic effects.

Dopamine (DA), as a catecholamine neurotransmitter in brain [24], is easily oxidized into o-benzoquinone (BQ) and quench the ECL of nano-illuminant [25]. Herein, the (111) facet-enclosed Pd nanocones (Pd NCs) were obtained by a one-pot poly-l-lysine (PLL)-mediated method under hydrothermal conditions (Scheme 1 A). Based on the ECL quenching mechanism, a sensitive and accurate detection of DA biosensor was designed on the basis of the ECL system employing Pd NCs as emitters, coupled with tripropylamine (TPA) as the coreactant. The possible enhanced ECL mechanism of Pd NCs with TPA and quenching mechanism of DA towards the Pd NCs/TPA system were discussed in this study (Scheme 1B). The above-operations illustrate that Pd NCs as a novel luminescent nanomaterial has the great prospect to enrich the ECL fundamental theory and application.

Chloropalladic acid (H2PdCl4), poly-l-lysine (PLL), and formaldehyde (HCHO) solution (40%) were supplied from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA), tripropylamine (TPA), hydrazine (HZ) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were purchased from Southeast Chemical Instrument Co., Ltd. (Jinhua, China). In the ECL measurement, phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was prepared with Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 (100 mM, pH 7.0). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Millipore water purification system (≥18 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore), and used throughout all the experiments if not stated otherwise.

The morphology and structure of the samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) on a JEOL JEM-2100 h transmission electron microscope (Japan Electronics Co., Ltd, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out by a K-Alpha XPS spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) attached with Al Kα X-ray radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) for excitation. UV–vis (UV–vis) transmission spectra were acquired with the Thermo nicolet evolution 500 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi general instrument Co., Ltd., China) in the wavelength region of 200–800 nm and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a FLS980 spectrofluorometer (EdinburghInstruments Co., Ltd., Britain).

The electrochemical and ECL experiments were performed with a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Inc., China) and an MPI-E multifunctional ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China), respectively. All the ECL and electrochemical measurements were conducted with a conventional three-electrode system, where a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 5 mm) served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode (filling liquid: saturated KCl) and a Pt wire acted as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. In addition, the ECL spectroscopy was realized spectroelectrochemically by galvanizing the 0.1 mg/mL Pd NCs and 30 mM TPA in detection buffer under hydrodynamic potentiostatic electrolysis at the potential of 1.6 V. All the potentials were quoted against Ag/AgCl electrode, if not specifically mentioned otherwise.

The Pd NCs were typically synthesized based on the previous literature [26] with a minor modification: 0.1280 g of PLL was dissolved into 10.75 mL of water under magnetic stirring, followed by putting 0.75 mL of the H2PdCl4 solution (100.0 mM). Next, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 11.0, and then 1.00 mL of the HCHO solution (37.0∼40.0 wt%) was added to the mixture. Then, the mixed solution was heated at 120 °C for 5 h in the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (20.00 mL). After being cooled to ambient temperature, the obtained Pd NCs were collected at 6000 rpm for 5 min by centrifugation for further characterization.

The GCE was cleaned firstly by polishing with 0.3 and 0.05 μm aluminum slurry sequentially, and then rinsed thoroughly with water to remove the residual alumina powder. Finally, the electrode was dried by N2 after ultrasonic washing. The well-polished electrode was then connected on a MPI-E ECL instrument between 0.0 and 1.4 V in 3 mL of the pH 7.0 PBS including 10 μg/mL Pd NCs, 30 mM TPA and diverse concentrations of DA for the ECL measurements. For the electrochemical tests, the GCE was treated in the similar way, and detected in 3 mL of the PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.5 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte.

The TEM and HR-TEM measurements are commonly conducted to characterize the morphological and structural features of metal nanocrystals [3]. As shown in Fig. 1 A, many uniform Pd nanoparticles are dispersed well in the product. The Pd particles have the well-defined triangular cone-like structures, with a mean side length of 60 nm (Fig. 1B), showing an obviously cubic nanocones [27]. Moreover, the cubic structures are further identified by the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1C), where many well-resolved lattice fringes appear with the interplanar spacing distances of 0.224 nm and 0.225 nm, in good coincidence with the (111) crystal planes of the face-centered cubic (fcc) Pd [28]. The surface and lattice structures synergistically confirm that Pd NCs are preferentially growing along the (111) directions.

Meanwhile, the surface composition and valence states of nanomaterials are usually investigated by XPS measurements [29]. The survey XPS spectrum of the product shows four major diffraction peaks located at 288.36, 311.18, 399.46, and 532.21 eV (Fig. 1D), which correspond to C, Pd, N and O elements [28], respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution Pd 3d XPS segment (Fig. 1E) can be separated into two pairs of peaks: the stronger couple at 343.02 and 337.68 eV is assigned to metallic Pd°, while the weaker pair at 343.76 and 338.51 eV refers to Pd2+ species. Clearly, metallic Pd° is the predominant species, indicating the efficient reduction of the Pd precursor in this synthesis, which would endow Pd NCs with improved electrocatalytic activity.

The optical performance of the obtained Pd NCs was conducted by a series of UV–vis and fluorescence (FL) measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, there is a wide absorption band (curve a) and the absorption inflection point shows up at around 336 nm closely associated with the classical metal resonance absorbance [30]. After the addition of TPA, the absorption peak intensity increases significantly (curve b), owing to the strong interactions between the amine groups in TPA and metal nanocrystals [31]. These observations manifest the efficient synthesis of the tiny and uniform Pd NCs by the current hydrothermal route. Moreover, the Pd NCs are strongly identified as an illuminant by the excitation-dependent FL emission spectrum of Pd NCs in the range of 280–400 nm [32]. As depicted by the inset in Fig. 2A, the PL intensities increase dramatically with the increase of the excitation wavelengths, reach the maximum at 434 nm, and then adversely decrease by further extending the wavelength.

Furthermore, the PL intensity of the Pd NCs/TPA system at 434 nm (curve b) is larger obviously than that of Pd NCs itself (curve a) by employing the excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 2B). Besides, the emission peak has a red shift from 434 to 437 nm with the largely decreased intensity (curve c) after the addition of DA into the above Pd NCs/TPA system.

The inset in Fig. 2B shows the ECL emission spectrum of Pd NCs with the help of optical filters. Compared to the PL spectrum, the emission peak exhibits a slight red-shift (from 434 to 466 nm) in the emission maxima, owing to the instrument effects and the difference in the excitation pathway [33]. To this end, the Pd NCs/TPA system is a luminescent body as expected, whose optical property is strongly affected by DA in this context [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The screening of coreactant and optimization of the detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: It is still necessary to search an efficient coreactant for Pd NCs in the anodic ECL system. Therefore, three types of coreactants (TPA, HZ and Na2C2O4) were carefully examined for the ECL responses of Pd NCs. In brief, the bare GCE was first detected in 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS containing 10 μg/mL Pd NCs. Then, the three coreatants were added into the testing buffer, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the ECL responses steeply increase from 80 to 8030 a.u. with the 100-fold enlargement in the intensity when the TPA is added to the testing buffer. The great enhancement in the ECL signals is attributed to the fast and efficient proton reaction between Pd NCs with the tert amino group in TPA, similar to that of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system in the literature [4]. However, negligible variation is noticed by using HZ or Na2C2O4 (Fig. 3B, curve c, d) as coreactant while keeping the other conditions identical, which is attributed to the fact that strong reducibility of HZ would destroy the structure of Pd NCs and C2O4 2− would hardly participate in the proton reaction. Thus, TPA is certified as the desired coreactant in this study.

Also, the experimental conditions are essential for the high sensitivity of the ECL systems [35]. Fig. 3C shows the ECL responses of the Pd NCs as a function of the TPA concentrations. Obviously, the ECL intensities are continuously enlarged as a function of the TPA concentrations, and reach a plateau roughly at 30 mM, due to the fact that more coreactants would produce more TPA•+, which would oxidize the Pd NCs to form active state Pd NCs *. However, the excess TPA would hinder the further reaction for the limited surface area of the electrode and spatial steric effect [36]. In this regard, 30 mM TPA is the optimal concentration for the sequential DA detection in this context.

As it is well recognized, the strong acid or alkaline conditions have large impacts on the electrochemical behaviors of molecule bioactivity, which is closely associated with the analytical performances of the ECL biosensor [37]. The ECL responses increase with the pH values from 5.0 to 7.0, and reach the maximum at 7.0, followed by the adverse decrease in the ECL intensity by further increasing the pH up to 8.0 (Fig. 3D). This is attributed to the inhibition of Pd NCs consumption and TPA deprotonation at the lower pH. However, highly alkaline surroundings would cause decomposition of some species, which would further induce an unstable system and weaken the ECL signals [35]. Thus, pH 7.0 PBS is selected as the detection electrolyte. The contrary experiments demonstrate that the ECL intensity of Pd NCs is obviously influenced by the concentration of TPA and the pH value of the electrolyte as expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible ECL reaction mechanism of Pd NCs/TPA and quenching of Pd NCs by DA
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optical studies of the Pd NCs and Pd NCs/TPA system, the possible ECL mechanism is deeply investigated by measuring the corresponding ECL signals (Fig. 4 A) and the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves (Fig. 4B). Specifically, the blank Pd NCs system has a weak ECL signal (about 80 a.u.) and a featureless voltammetric profile with low currents (curve a). After the addition of TPA, there is a strong ECL emission intensity (8030 a.u.) with largely boosted oxidation peak at 0.95 V (curve b). We also investigate the ECL and electrochemical behaviors of the coreactant TPA as a contrast experiment (insets in Fig. 4). Impressively, negligible ECL emission is found and the homologous CV current is also too weak to be discovered, and thereby the ECL signal of TAP itself is effectively excluded.

According to the aforementioned ECL and CV experimental data, the ECL mechanism of the Pd NCs/TPA system is in good coincidence with that of Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system [38]: First, Pd NCs lose electrons on the electrode surface to form Pd NCs+ (Eq. (1)), followed by the immediate oxidation of TPA to generate strongly reducing species of TPA · group through the loss of a proton (Eqs. (2) and (3) [39]. Next, the freshly-formed Pd NCs+ react with TPA · to produce the excited state of Pd NCs* via energy transformation (Eq. (4)). Finally, a strong ECL emission would occur at 466 nm by the return of the excited state of Pd NCs* to Pd NCs again (Eq. (5)). On account of the ECL mechanism, the reaction rate of TPA ·
+ electron-generated and electron transfer are the key factors to the ECL emission of Pd NCs in the Pd NCs/TPA system. The process is roughly outlined as the following equations:
(1) Pd NCs - e− → Pd NCs+
(2) TPA - e− → TPA
+
(3) TPA
+ - H+ → TPA
(4) Pd NCs+ + TPA
→ Pd NCs * + P1
(5) Pd NCs * → Pd NCs + hv (λem = 466 nm)

As observed obviously in Fig. 4A (curve c), the ECL intensity of Pd NCs is significantly weak using DA as the quencher for the Pd NCs/TPA system. Specifically, the ECL intensities are decreased from 8030 to 2340 a.u. by the addition of DA into the above system. Additionally, there is a prominent reversible redox peaks (0.23/0.12 V, Fig. 4B, curve c), accompanied by the almost disappearance of the oxidation peak at 0.95 V because of the remarkable decrease of TPA. The DA oxidation equation is described as below:
DA - e- → BQ (at 0.23 V)

Taken together, the ECL quenching mechanism of Pd NCs is ascribed as following: DA is easily oxidized to produce BQ at 0.23 V, which would quench the excited state of Pd NCs* by blocking-up the electron migration and energy transfer of Pd NCs [18]. The possible ECL reaction mechanism of Pd NCs/TPA and quenching mechanism of DA to Pd NCs are sketched in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL performance of the Pd NCs/TPA towards DA
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the enhanced stable ECL emission of the Pd NCs/TPA, a facile analytical method was developed for DA detection based on the ECL quenching mechanism. Herein, the ECL intensity of the Pd NCs/TPA system was monitored in PBS containing different concentrations of DA under the optimal conditions. As exhibited in Fig. 5 A, the ECL intensities have the large decay with the increase of the DA concentrations. More specifically, Fig. 5B displays a good linear relationship between the ECL signals and the logarithm of the DA concentrations ranging from 1 pM to 10 nM. The linear regression equation is described as I = -1448lg(c/M) -10935 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995, where I is the ECL intensity of the analysis model, and c represents the concentration of DA. Meanwhile, the limit of detection is calculated to be 0.46 pM (using 3σ method), which is comparable or even smaller than those previous biosensors [37,40,41]. As shown in Table 1 , the as-fabricated ECL biosensor exhibits comparable or even higher sensitivity alternative to those in the literature. The enhanced ECL performances are mainly ascribed to the superior optical properties of Pd NCs.

In order to further assess the performance of the ECL biosensor for the assay of DA, the selectivity was studied by using the potential interfering substances such as l-arginine (L-Arg), glucose (Glu), histidine (His), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glycine (Gly). Fig. 5C exhibits the variation in the ECL intensity of the sensor in the presence of 0.1 nM DA or 10.0 nM different interfering chemicals (e.g., l-Arg, Glc, His, AA, UA, and Glu). The ECL intensities have the distinguishable changes at the ultra-low DA concentration (0.1 nM) when compared to those of the interferences even with the much higher concentrations (100-fold, 10 nM) under the identical operation conditions. It reflects the highest selectivity of the ECL sensor among the investigated substances for the determination of DA. Meanwhile, the stability was also investigated by the consecutive cyclic scanning. As displayed in Fig. 5D, the ECL intensities are almost constant by continuous potential sweeping, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.9%, showing the satisfactory precision and acceptable reliability of the ECL system.

In summary, Pd NCs were efficiently prepared under the hydrothermal conditions by utilizing PLL as the green protecting ligands. The high-efficient ECL illuminant of Pd NCs and the anodic ECL emission were first demonstrated in the aqueous system. For this emitter, TPA was identified as the best coreactant to enhance the ECL intensity. This was ascribed to the oxidation of TPA to form strong reducing species that would react with Pd NCs, ultimately generating the excited state Pd NCs* with strong irradiation at 466 nm via the energy transfer route. Based on the excellent ECL luminophore of the Pd NCs/TPA system, a novel "signal-off" strategy was designed for the DA detection based on the ECL quenching mechanism. The ECL sensor had the low detection limit down to 0.46 pM and high selectivity. Metallic architectures were confirmed as promising luminescent candidates for conventional “metal to ligand” mode, which would enrich the ECL fundamental theory and show promising applications in the ECL field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475118 and 21675093), Basic Public Welfare Research Project of Zhejiang Province (No. LGG18E010001), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201703, STAM201804), the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education for Advanced Catalysis Materials (Zhejiang Normal University), and PhD Fund of Zhejiang Normal University for Young Teachers (Nos. zz323205020517002104 and ZZ323205020518001924).

Nonenzymatic sensing of glucose using a glassy carbon electrode modified with halloysite nanotubes heavily loaded with palladium nanoparticles

One-pot, water-based and high-yield synthesis of tetrahedral palladium nanocrystal decorated graphene
Ratiometric electrochemiluminescent strategy regulated by electrocatalysis of palladium nanocluster for immunosensing
Electrochemical synthesis of very stable photoluminescent copper clusters
Recent advances based on nanomaterials as electrochemiluminescence probes for the fabrication of sensors
Copper nanoclusters as a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for ferric ions in serum and living cells by imaging
Highly efficient electrogenerated chemiluminescence of Au38 nanoclusters
Potential-modulated electrochemiluminescence of carbon nitride nanosheets for dual-signal sensing of metal ions
Determination of pentachlorophenol by anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as co-reactant
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence behavior of graphite-like carbon nitride and its application in selective sensing Cu2+
A novel ECL biosensor for the detection of concanavalin A based on glucose functionalized NiCo2S4 nanoparticles-grown on carboxylic graphene as quenching probe
Ru(bpy)3 2+ incorporated luminescent polymer dots: double-enhanced electrochemiluminescence for detection of single-nucleotide polymorphism
CdZnTeS quantum dots based electrochemiluminescent image immunoanalysis
Stepwise chemical reaction strategy for highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent detection of dopamine
Surface-enhanced molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor based on Ru@SiO2 for ultrasensitive detection of fumonisin B1
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine

A double signal amplification platform for ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid and acetaminophen based on a nanocomposite of ferrocene thiolate stabilized Fe3O4@Au nanoparticles with graphene sheet
Silver nanoprobe for sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of dopamine via robust Ag-catechol interaction

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hui-Min Wang received her bachelor’s degree in Department of chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Huaiyin Normal University. Currently, she is a postgraduate in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University. Her research interests are Electrochemiluminescence, functional nanomaterials, and bioanalysis.

Chen-Chen Wang is an undergraduate in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. Her research interests are functional nanomaterials, electrochemical sensor and Electrochemiluminescence.

Ai-Jun Wang is a professor in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2007. His research interests include electrochemical sensor, functional nanomaterials and biomaterials.

Lu Zhang is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Hiroshima University in 2017. Her research interests include Raman, functional nanomaterials and fuel cells.

Xiliang Luo received his PhD degree from Nanjing University in 2005. He then worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Dublin City University, Arizona State University and the University of Pittsburgh successively. In 2011, he became a research assistant professor in the Department of Bioengineering, University of Pittsburgh and a senior Marie Curie Fellow in the Department of Chemistry, University of Oxford. He then joined the Qingdao University of Science and Technology at the end of 2011as a Taishan Scholar professor. His scientific interests are focused on biochemical analysis, nanobiosensing, electrochemistry and conducting polymers.

Pei-Xin Yuan is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University of Science and Technology in 2017. His research interests include electrochemical (bio)sensors, Electrochemiluminescence, functional nanomaterials and biomaterials.

Jiu-Ju Feng is a professor in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2006. She did her post-doctoral research in Technische Universität Berlin. Her research interests include electrochemical (bio)sensors, Raman and fluorescence spectroscopy, functional nanomaterials and biomaterials, as well as fuel cells.
